t: Put these in order from least to greatest: 899, 908, 890, 981
e: Compare hundreds: all have 8 or 9 hundreds. 890 (8h, 9t) < 899 (8h, 9t, 9o) < 908 (9h) < 981 (9h, 8t). Order: 890, 899, 908, 981.

t: Sam says 452 > 459 because 5 > 4. Is Sam right? What did he do wrong?
o: He compared hundreds instead of ones
He compared random digits instead of going place by place
He compared tens when he should compare ones
e: Both numbers have 4 hundreds and 5 tens. The difference is in the ones: 2 vs 9. Since 2 < 9, 452 < 459. Sam should have compared ones since hundreds and tens matched.

t: Nina made the greatest 3-digit number and the least 3-digit number using digits 5, 0, and 8 (each once). What is the difference?
e: Greatest: put biggest digit in hundreds: 850. Least 3-digit: cannot start with 0, so put 5 in hundreds: 508. Difference: 850 - 508 = 342.

t: Which shows the numbers in order from greatest to least?
e: All have 1 thousand and 1 or 0 hundreds. 1,120 has 1 hundred and 2 tens. 1,102 has 1 hundred and 0 tens. 1,012 has 0 hundreds. So: 1,120 > 1,102 > 1,012.

t: Kai says 399 is greater than 400 because 3 + 9 + 9 = 21 and 4 + 0 + 0 = 4. What is wrong with his thinking?
o: You compare place by place, not add digits
You should multiply digits, not add
The digits are too big to add
e: Adding digits does not tell you which number is greater. Compare place by place: 399 has 3 hundreds, 400 has 4 hundreds. Since 3 < 4, 399 < 400.

t: Using the digits 6, 2, and 9 (each used once), what is the LEAST 3-digit number you can make?
e: For the least number, put the smallest digit in hundreds: 2. Then next smallest in tens: 6. Then 9 in ones. Least = 269.

t: Three friends measured their jumps. Ava: 108 cm, Ben: 180 cm, Cal: 118 cm. Put them in order from shortest to longest jump.
e: 108 < 118 < 180. Shortest to longest: Ava (108), Cal (118), Ben (180).

t: Mia says that since 59 > 51, then 598 > 519 is also true. Is her reasoning correct?
o: Yes, comparing the first two digits works
No, you must compare all three digits starting from hundreds
e: Both have 5 hundreds, so compare tens: 9 > 1, so 598 > 519. Mia got the right answer. Comparing the first two digits can work when hundreds match, but the proper method is comparing place by place from left to right.

t: What is the smallest number that is greater than 1,199?
e: The very next number after 1,199 is 1,200. So 1,200 is the smallest number greater than 1,199.

t: Tyler has cards showing 4, 7, and 0. He wants to make a 3-digit number between 400 and 500. Which numbers can he make?
e: Between 400 and 500 means hundreds digit = 4. The remaining digits are 7 and 0. He can make 407 or 470. Both are between 400 and 500.

t: Put these in order from least to greatest: 1,200, 1,020, 1,002, 1,100
e: All have 1 thousand. Compare hundreds: 1,002 (0h), 1,020 (0h), 1,100 (1h), 1,200 (2h). For the two with 0 hundreds, compare tens: 1,002 (0t) < 1,020 (2t). Order: 1,002, 1,020, 1,100, 1,200.

t: Eva says 789 < 798 < 879 < 897. Is she correct?
e: Check each pair: 789 < 798 (same hundreds, 8 < 9 in tens). 798 < 879 (7 hundreds < 8 hundreds). 879 < 897 (same hundreds, 7 < 9 in tens). All correct!

t: A number is greater than 550 but less than 560. Its ones digit is odd. Its digits add up to 13. What is it?
e: Between 550 and 560 with odd ones: 551, 553, 555, 557, 559. Check digit sums: 5+5+1=11, 5+5+3=13, 5+5+5=15, 5+5+7=17, 5+5+9=19. Only 553 has digits that add to 13.

t: Leo and Zoe each made a 3-digit number. Leo's number has 8 hundreds and is less than 830. Zoe's has 8 hundreds and is greater than 870. Whose number is greater?
e: Leo's number is less than 830, so it could be 800-829. Zoe's is greater than 870, so it could be 871-899. Any number Zoe could have is greater than any number Leo could have.

t: Which set of numbers is in order from GREATEST to LEAST?
e: Greatest to least means biggest first: 543 > 435 > 345. Compare hundreds: 5 > 4 > 3.

t: Dana says you only need to look at the hundreds digit to compare 738 and 742. Is she right?
o: No, both have 7 hundreds so you must check tens too
No, you always check ones first
Yes, because 7 = 7 means they are equal
e: Both numbers have 7 hundreds, so hundreds alone cannot tell us. We must compare tens: 738 has 3 tens, 742 has 4 tens. Since 3 < 4, 738 < 742.

t: What is the greatest 3-digit number where no digit repeats and the hundreds digit is even?
e: Hundreds must be even, so the biggest even digit for hundreds is 8. Then tens = 9 (biggest remaining), ones = 7 (next biggest). Answer: 897.

t: Nora wrote: 401 < 410 < 400. Find the error.
o: 400 should come first since it is least
e: 400 < 401 < 410. Nora put 400 at the end (as if it were greatest), but 400 is the least of the three.

t: I am between 990 and 1,010. I am an even number. My digits add up to 9. What number am I?
Three kids ran a race. Times: Ava 1,050 seconds, Ben 1,005 seconds, Cho 1,500 seconds. Who was fastest (least time)?
e: Fastest means least time. 1,005 < 1,050 < 1,500. Ben had the least time (1,005 seconds), so he was fastest.
